coredata.dc:description: Accurate and rapid monitoring of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) residues is crucial for regulating food safety. Herein, dual-emission carbon dots (de-CDs) were fabricated for the ratiometric detection of OPs and Hg2+. The de-CDs exhibited two emission peaks at 678 and 485 nm when excited with visible light. Interestingly, the fluorescence at 678 nm was significantly quenched by Hg2+ mainly because of the static quenching effect, whereas that at 485 nm exhibited a slight change. More significantly, the quenched fluorescence of the de-CDs recovered remarkably after introducing omethoate, diazinon and malathion. Accordingly, the ratiometric detection of the three OPs and Hg2+ was achieved with high selectivity and robust performance. In addition, the OPs residues assay in Brassica chinensis was successfully performed with satisfactory results. This study not only provides an attractive tool for the simple and rapid assay of OPs but also offers new insights into the fabrication of multi-functional carbon dots.

head.ce:title: pH-responsive dual-emission carbon dots for the ratiometric detection of organophosphorus pesticides in Brassica chinensis and Hg2+ in water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate and rapid monitoring of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) residues is crucial for regulating food safety. Herein, dual-emission carbon dots (de-CDs) were fabricated for the ratiometric detection of OPs and Hg2+. The de-CDs exhibited two emission peaks at 678 and 485 nm when excited with visible light. Interestingly, the fluorescence at 678 nm was significantly quenched by Hg2+ mainly because of the static quenching effect, whereas that at 485 nm exhibited a slight change. More significantly, the quenched fluorescence of the de-CDs recovered remarkably after introducing omethoate, diazinon and malathion. Accordingly, the ratiometric detection of the three OPs and Hg2+ was achieved with high selectivity and robust performance. In addition, the OPs residues assay in Brassica chinensis was successfully performed with satisfactory results. This study not only provides an attractive tool for the simple and rapid assay of OPs but also offers new insights into the fabrication of multi-functional carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food contamination caused by pesticides, heavy metal ions, pathogens, viruses or bacteria has attracted widespread attention worldwide. Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), a catalogue of efficient, rapid and relatively low-persistence pesticides, have been extensively used in agricultural areas to eradicate pests in recent decades (Kaur & Singh, 2020; Ma et al., 2020). However, the long-term and excessive use of OPs inevitably leads to residues in vegetables, fruits and environmental media such as water resources and soil (He et al., 2021). OPs can inhibit the activity of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) by interacting with serine on AChE, which inevitably interrupts neurotransmitter transmission in organisms (Cai et al., 2021; Dong et al., 2023). Accordingly, several countries and organisations have established the maximum residue levels (MRLs) of OPs. For instance, the European Union and the China Food Safety Organisation proposed the MRL values of 0.05 mgꞏkg−1 for parathion and 0.2 to 1 mgꞏkg−1 for acephate in fresh vegetables and fruits, respectively (Cai et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). In addition, Hg2+, one of the most toxic heavy metal ions, tends to accumulate in organisms and cause various diseases (Guo et al., 2019; Wu & Tong, 2020; Yuen et al., 2014). The intake of Hg2+ by organisms mainly occurs through contaminated water and agricultural products (Yuen et al., 2014). Therefore, it is crucial to achieve regular, rapid and effective monitoring of OPs and Hg2+ in food substrates and environmental water.

Traditional analytical techniques for OPs include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Seebunrueng, Santaladchaiyakit, & Srijaranai, 2014), gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) (Zhang et al., 2016), liquid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry (LC–MS) (Yu et al., 2020) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Su et al., 2022). Despite its superior sensitivity, chromatography requires sophisticated instrumentation, and GC–MS, in particular, involves a time-consuming pre-treatment process. ELISA suffers from poor stability of antibodies and antigens because of its susceptibility to external environments (Cai et al., 2021). In recent years, several novel technologies for OPs analysis have emerged to compensate for the shortcomings of the aforementioned methods, including electrochemical assay (He et al., 2021; Wu, Yang, Li, Li, & Li, 2021), chemi-luminescence (Li, He, & Xu, 2007), fluorescence analysis (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022), AChE inhibitory biosensors (Wu et al., 2021; Yang, Li, Li, & Li, 2021; Yao et al., 2019) and enzyme-catalysed biosensors (Kaur & Singh, 2020; Tang et al., 2014). Among these methods, fluorescence assays have attracted extensive attention because of their simplicity, rapidity and visualisation.

Numerous fluorescent probes based on carbon dots (Li et al., 2020; Vadia et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2017), quantum dots (Liu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021) and metal nano-clusters (Cai et al., 2021; Li, Wu, Yang, Li, & Li, 2021) are applied for OPs detection. Despite the great progress, several issues should be of concern. First, numerous emitters suffer from poor stability and dispersibility in an aqueous solution. Second, multiple probes usually require the introduction of additional enzymes or aptamers, which leads to cumbersome fabrication processes and high costs. In addition, the introduced enzymes and hydrolysates probably affect the probe's stability (Cai et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2019). Third, regardless of the distinctive advantages of carbon dots, including ease of synthesis, excellent photostability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, most carbon dots exhibit a single short-wavelength emission, which is usually interfered by fluorescence from the sample matrix (Li et al., 2020). Furthermore, sensing strategies based on single emission are susceptible to external environmental and instrumental conditions, resulting in poor accuracy (Wu et al., 2023).

In this study, intrinsic dual-emission carbon dots (de-CDs) were developed for the ratiometric detection of OPs and Hg2+. As depicted in Scheme 1 , de-CDs with two fluorescence signals at 678 and 485 nm were synthesised using formamide and glutathione as precursors. When Hg2+ was introduced, fluorescence at 678 nm was significantly quenched, accompanied by a slight change in fluorescence at 485 nm. More interestingly, after the introduction of three OPs, namely omethoate, diazinon and malathion, de-CDs were released from the de-CD–Hg2+ complex owing to the competitive coordination of OPs with Hg2+, resulting in the remarkable fluorescence recovery of de-CDs. Accordingly, the ratiometric determination of the three OPs and Hg2+ was achieved based on de-CDs. Furthermore, common interfering substances, including ions, small molecules and other OPs, did not interfere with the detection results. Finally, the proposed probe was successfully applied for the assay of OPs residues in Brassica chinensis and Hg2+ in water samples.

Ten pesticides, namely omethoate, diazinon, malathion, acephate, dipterex, parathion-methyl, dichlorvos, phoxim, chlorpyrifos and methomyl, were purchased from Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH (Augsburg, Germany). Tetracycline, formamide, sodium hydrogen phosphate and tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane hydrochloride (Tris-HCl) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, glucose, l-glutamic acid, l-alanine, l-leucine and l-lysine were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other salts were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Regents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The seeds of Brassica chinensis were purchased from Jiangsu Yechun Seed Industry Co., Ltd. (China). De-ionized water (18.2 MΩꞏcm) was used throughout the experiment.

The morphology of de-CDs was characterised using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, German). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Nicolet IS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha + spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an F-7000 spectrofluorometer with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 700 V (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis spectra were recorded using a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence lifetime decay curves and absolute fluorescence quantum yields (FLQYs) were recorded using an FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh, England). Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP–MS) NexION 350Xi (PerkinElmer, USA) was used for sample analysis.

de-CDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal process according to a previously reported method with modifications (Gao et al., 2023). In detail, in a 50-mL beaker, 0.4 g of glutathione was dispersed in 15 mL of formamide. The mixed solution was ultra-sonicated for 30 min to obtain a transparent solution, which was then transferred to a 25-mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After heating at 180 °C for 5 h, a black–green product solution was obtained. The product was purified using a dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 7 days to remove the residual precursors. Finally, the product after dialysis was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min), and the collected de-CDs aqueous solution was subjected to lyophilisation to obtain the de-CDs powder. In addition, de-CDs were prepared with 15 mL of formamide and other concentrations of glutathione (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.6, 0.8 and 1.0 g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric determination of Hg2+ and OPs using de-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The de-CDs powder was re-dispersed in de-ionized water. For Hg2+ detection, the test samples were prepared as follows: 200 μL of the de-CDs solution (0.1 g⋅L−1) and 50 μL of sodium hydrogen phosphate–citric acid buffer (200 mM, pH 5.4) were added to 5-mL test tubes. Then, Hg2+ at different concentrations (0–2.4 μM) was added. Finally, the mixed solution was diluted to 1 mL with de-ionized water and thoroughly mixed. The test samples for OPs analysis were obtained as follows: In a 5-mL test tube, 50 μL of sodium hydrogen phosphate–citric acid buffer (200 mM, pH 5.4), 200 μL of the de-CDs solution (0.1 g⋅L−1), 200 μL of Hg2+ (10 μM) and OPs at different concentrations were added. All samples were diluted to 1 mL, thoroughly mixed and allowed to stand at room temperature for 30 min. The fluorescence spectra of all samples were recorded using a spectrofluorometer with an excitation wavelength of 416 nm and excitation and emission slits of 5 nm. The ratiometric detection of Hg2+ and OPs was achieved by establishing an expression between the concentration of Hg2+ or OPs and the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 678 and 485 nm (F
678
/F
485 ). For selectivity tests, interfering substances such as ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Ag+, Cr3+, Pb2+, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, Ac−, NO2 −, SO3 2−, F−, Cl−, Br− and I−), small molecules (ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, tetracycline, citric acid, glucose, l-glutamic acid, l-alanine, l-leucine and l-lysine) and other common pesticides were added instead of Hg2+ or OPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of Hg2+ and OPs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The Hg2+ assay in water samples was examined using the proposed fluorescent probe. Two water samples were collected from the campus lake and the laboratory tap water. The lake water was purified by centrifugation (5000 rpm, 10 min), and the obtained supernatant was filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane. The pre-treated lake water and tap water were spiked with Hg2+ (20, 200 and 1000 nM), and the spiked samples were analysed using the probe.

For the analysis of OPs in real samples, omethoate was chosen as a representative to evaluate the potential applicability of the proposed fluorescent probe. Specifically, the seeds of Brassica chinensis were cultivated in the soil under sunlight at a temperature of 15 °C–25 °C and sprayed with water every 5 days; therefore, two batches of Brassica chinensis were cultivated. After 15 days of cultivation, one group was sprayed with an omethoate solution (5 mgꞏmL−1) every 3 days for 10 times. The other group that was sprayed with water served as the control. After the last spray, the residual omethoate in the leaves and roots of Brassica chinensis was determined using the proposed probe. Specifically, 10 g of leaves or roots were cut into small pieces, and 10 mL of methanol was added. After shaking at room temperature for 12 h, the extracts were subjected to centrifugation (7154×g, 10 min), and a supernatant was collected. Finally, the omethoate content in the extraction solution was analysed using the probe. In addition, the OPs concentration in Brassica chinensis was also evaluated using HPLC. A DIONEX UltiMate 3000 HPLC system from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MY, USA) equipped with a Zorbax SB-C18 column (4.6 mm × 250 mm i.d., 5 μm; Agilent, Palo Alto, CA, USA) was used. The temperature of the column was maintained at 25 °C, and the injection volume was 10 μL. The mobile phase consisted of 55% water (A) and 45% methanol (B) with a flow rate of 1 mLꞏmin−1.

As shown in Scheme 1, de-CDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal process, and the optimisation of de-CDs synthesis is provided in the Supplementary Materials (Fig. S1 and Table S1). The micromorphology of de-CDs was characterised by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the corresponding results are shown in Figs. 1a and S2a. de-CDs exhibited good mono-dispersibility in the aqueous solution, and the size distribution histogram indicated that the diameters of de-CDs were in the range of 1.5–5.0 nm, with an average size of 3.0 ± 0.4 nm. Furthermore, no crystal stripes were observed in the high-resolution TEM images of de-CDs. The XRD pattern shows a broad and extremely weak diffraction peak at 25.4°, confirming the amorphous structure of de-CDs, which agrees well with the high-resolution TEM results (Fig. S2b). Functional groups on the surface of de-CDs were characterised using the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1b). The broad stretching vibration centred at 3435 cm−1 is assigned to ν(O–H/N–H) of carboxyl/amide groups, and the peak at 1683 cm−1 is assigned to ν(C=O) of carboxyl groups (Li et al., 2020). The presence of these groups contributes to the hydrophilicity and stability of de-CDs in an aqueous solution. The adsorption band at 2358 cm−1 corresponds to ν(CS), and the peak at 1388 cm−1 is assigned to ν(C–O/C–N), demonstrating the existence of N- and S-containing groups in de-CDs (Li et al., 2012). Furthermore, the stretching vibration at 1594 cm−1 corresponds to the conjugate structure of ν(C=C) (Jia, Li, & Wang, 2012). The above results confirm the successful doping of N and S in de-CDs.

The chemical element composition and valence state of de-CDs were further investigated by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1c, the four peaks observed in the XPS spectrum indicate the existence of C, N, O and S, and their atomic percentage (At.%) is 59.22, 20.23, 19.55 and 0.99, respectively. In addition, high-resolution XPS images of C, N, O and S were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1d, the high-resolution C1s spectrum of de-CDs is de-convoluted into five types of carbon bonds: sp2 CC (283.88 eV), CC (284.78 eV), CN (285.48 eV), CO (286.88 eV) and CO (287.78 eV) (Wang et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2023). The high-resolution N1s spectrum is fitted into two binding peaks at 399.08 and 400.28 eV, corresponding to CN and NH, respectively (Fig. 1e) (Wu et al., 2023; Wu & Tong, 2020). In the high-resolution O1s spectrum, the fitted binding peaks at 530.88, 531.78 and 532.98 eV are ascribed to C–OH, CO and SO, respectively (Fig. 1f) (Wang et al., 2019). Four binding peaks at 168.28, 164.58, 163.48 and 161.98 eV de-convoluted from the high-resolution S1s spectrum correspond to SO, S
2p2/1 C–S–C, S
2p3/2 C–S–C and SO, respectively (Fig. 1g) (Dong et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2019). In summary, these results confirm the successful preparation of de-CDs with abundant functional groups.

In addition, the optical properties of de-CDs were studied by recording their UV–vis absorption spectra and excitation and emission spectra in the aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 1h (blue line), de-CDs apparently exhibit a broad absorption band in the 300–500 nm region, and a maximum absorption peak is observed at 416 nm. The strong absorption in the visible light region is mainly attributed to the n–π* transitions of heteroatom-containing bonds in de-CDs, such as CO, CN and SO (Wang et al., 2019). Furthermore, the recorded excitation spectrum of de-CDs agrees well with their absorption spectrum (Fig. 1h, left red line). Therefore, it can be easily assumed that the fluorescence signal of de-CDs can be collected under visible light, which will contribute to their fluorescence sensing applications by reducing the interference from background fluorescence in the sample matrix. Furthermore, diversified emission traps originating from multiple functional groups and defect-state emission stemming from heteroatom-containing groups, at least to some extent, offer the possibility of double or multiple emission signals in de-CDs (Bao, Liu, Zhang, & Pang, 2015). As expected, under an excitation wavelength of 416 nm, the emission spectrum was obtained with two fluorescence peaks at 485 and 678 nm (Fig. 1h, right red line), and bright pink fluorescence can be observed with the naked eye under 365-nm UV light (inset in Fig. 1h). To further demonstrate the successful fabrication of de-CDs with two emission signals, the fluorescence spectra of de-CDs at different concentrations were examined. As shown in Fig. 1i, both emission signals at 485 and 678 nm gradually enhanced with the same increasing regulation as the concentration of de-CDs increased from 0.005 to 0.05 gꞏL−1. Furthermore, the emission intensity at 485 and 678 nm almost reached the maximum value simultaneously at a concentration of 0.05 gꞏL−1. These results confirm that the two fluorescence signals originated from de-CDs.

More significantly, no obvious photobleaching was observed in de-CDs even after 6 months of storage in air at room temperature, demonstrating their excellent optical stability. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of de-CDs were examined at different pH values in the aqueous solution. Regardless of the Tris-HCl buffer solution or the sodium hydrogen phosphate–citric acid buffer solution, de-CDs exhibited good optical stability under weakly acidic conditions, i.e. pH values in the range of 5.0–7.0. However, the fluorescence at 678 nm became weaker in a strongly acidic environment (Fig. S3a, b). More interestingly, the emission peak at 678 nm shifted to 645 nm at pH 7.5–10, and its intensity gradually increased with increasing pH. This significant change is probably due to the presence of numerous heteroatoms and functional groups on the surface of de-CDs. Their zeta potential and protonation can change correspondingly at different pH values (Li et al., 2021). In summary, these results demonstrate that pH-responsive de-CDs were successfully synthesised and could have promising applications in pH measurement or target sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing principle of de-CDs for Hg2+ and OPs
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from the aforementioned results, de-CDs possess dual emissions centred at 678 and 485 nm. Next, the possible sensing application of de-CDs was investigated. Surprisingly, upon incorporation of Hg2+, the fluorescence at 678 nm was significantly quenched, whereas that at 485 nm showed only a negligible change (Fig. 2a). Furthermore, the quenched fluorescence at 678 nm recovered when omethoate, diazinon or malathion was introduced into the as-obtained de-CD–Hg2+ system (Fig. 2b–d). Therefore, the ratiometric detection of Hg2+ and OPs can be achieved based on the dual-emission signals of de-CDs without further functionalisation.

In general, fluorescence quenching of fluorophores can be achieved through a dynamic quenching effect (interaction of quenchers with fluorophores in the excited state through electron or energy transfer), a static quenching effect (interaction of quenchers with fluorophores in the ground state to form a non-fluorescent complex) or both simultaneously (Tan, Zhang, & Chen, 2011). The apparent shortening of the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophores usually occurs during dynamic quenching. In contrast, the fluorescence lifetime of fluorophores remains almost unchanged in the static quenching mechanism. Both dynamic and static quenching effects can be described using the Stern–Volmer equation (i.e. Eq.
1 ).

where F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore before and after the addition of quenching species, respectively.

To study the quenching mechanism of de-CDs at 678 nm by Hg2+, the UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence lifetime decay curve of de-CDs before and after the addition of Hg2+ at different concentrations were determined. As shown in Fig. 2e, the absorption intensity of de-CDs gradually decreased after the introduction of Hg2+ in the range of 0.5–2.0 gꞏL−1. This is probably because there are numerous electronegative functional groups, i.e. oxygen- and sulfide-containing groups, on the surface of de-CDs, which easily combine with Hg2+ to form the de-CD–Hg2+ complex. Accordingly, the significant decrease in the absorption intensity of de-CDs after interaction with Hg2+ leads to significant fluorescence quenching. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime decay curve and fitted fluorescence lifetime of de-CDs at 678 nm showed negligible changes after adding Hg2+ at different concentrations (Figs. 2f, S4a and S4c–e). These results confirm that static quenching is a prominent factor in the Hg2+-induced fluorescence quenching of de-CDs. In addition, the Stern–Volmer equation was used to study the quenching process of de-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2g, an obvious non-linear Stern–Volmer curve was obtained between the F
0
/F and Hg2+ concentrations (C
Hg
2+ ). The upwards curvature demonstrates that dynamic and static quenching effects occurred simultaneously in the quenching process of de-CDs by Hg2+ (Lakowicz, 2006; Lu et al., 2018). Furthermore, an equivalent decrease in F
0
/F and τ
0
/τ can be achieved in the dynamic quenching process, where τ0 and τ are the fluorescence lifetimes of the fluorophores before and after interaction with the quenchers, respectively. Nevertheless, with the addition of 2.0 μM Hg2+ into the de-CDs probe, the value of F
0
/F = 6.4 is much higher than that of τ
0
/τ = 1.0. Therefore, the static quenching effect is a predominant mechanism in the Hg2+-induced fluorescence quenching of de-CDs (Li et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2011).

The Hg2+-quenched fluorescence of de-CDs can be restored by introducing three OPs (omethoate, diazinon or malathion) into the de-CD–Hg2+ system. The reason for this phenomenon lies in the competitive combination of the OPs with Hg2+ via their sulfide-containing groups. As shown in Fig. S5a–c, the absorption intensity of de-CDs in the de-CD–Hg2+ system increased significantly after the addition of the OPs. Furthermore, compared with the aggregated state of the de-CD–Hg2+ system, excellent mono-dispersibility of de-CDs was observed after the introduction of the OPs (Fig. 2h, i). Accordingly, the addition of the OPs released de-CDs from their coordination state with Hg2+, resulting in the fluorescence restoration of de-CDs.

To improve the performance of the ratiometric detection of Hg2+ and OPs, various conditions were optimised. It is worth noting that because of the slight change at 485 nm in de-CDs, only the effect of possible factors on the fluorescence at 678 nm was investigated. The fluorescence intensity ratio, i.e. (F
0 − F)/F
0 or (F − F
0
)/F
0 , was used to evaluate the response efficiency, where F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of de-CDs or de-CD–Hg2+ complex at 678 nm before and after the addition of Hg2+ or malathion, respectively. First, the concentration of de-CDs was optimised. As the concentration increased, the fluorescence of both de-CDs and the de-CD–Hg2+ complex at 678 nm gradually increased, regardless of whether Hg2+ or malathion was added (Fig. S6a). However, the values of both (F
0 − F)/F
0 of de-CDs and (F − F
0
)/F
0 of the de-CD–Hg2+ complex decreased because of the increasing free de-CDs in the aqueous solution (Fig. S6b, c). Accordingly, considering the fluorescence intensity of de-CDs and the response efficiency, 0.02 gꞏL−1 de-CDs is chosen. Next, the effect of C
Hg
2+ was examined. With increasing C
Hg
2+ , the malathion-induced fluorescence recovery efficiency gradually improved (Fig. S7a, b). Furthermore, when Hg2+ was 2.0 μM, the fluorescence de-CDs at 678 nm was almost completely quenched, and the (F
0 − F)/F
0 value of the de-CD–Hg2+ complex increased significantly after the incorporation of malathion. Therefore, 2.0 μM Hg2+ was used to fabricate the de-CD–Hg2+ complex for the OPs assay. In addition, considering the hydrolysis of Hg2+ and decomposition of OPs under alkaline conditions, the effect of pH values in the range of 3.0–7.0 was investigated. As shown in Fig. S8a–c, both the response efficiency of de-CDs to Hg2+ and the de-CD–Hg2+ complex to malathion increased with pH from 4.0 to 6.6. However, the fluorescence intensity of the de-CD–Hg2+ complex after the inclusion of malathion decreased remarkably when the pH exceeded 5.4. This is possible because of the partial hydrolysis of Hg2+, which results in its weak coordination with malathion. Accordingly, a pH of 5.4 was chosen. Finally, the response efficiency of de-CDs to Hg2+ and the de-CD–Hg2+ complex to malathion reached a stable level in 10 s and 10 min (Fig. S9a, b), respectively. Thus, an optimised equilibration time for Hg2+ and OPs detection was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric detection of Hg2+ and OPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimised conditions, the performance of the de-CDs for the ratiometric determination of Hg2+ was examined. Specifically, the fluorescence response of de-CDs to Hg2+ at different concentrations (0.02–2.4 μM) was recorded, and the results are depicted in Fig. 3a. The emission signal of de-CDs at 678 nm gradually decreased with an increase in C
Hg
2+ , whereas the fluorescence at 485 nm showed a slight change. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity ratios at 678 and 485 nm, i.e. F
678
/F
485 , was calculated. As a result, the value of F
678
/F
485 decreased with increasing C
Hg
2+ . Furthermore, F
678
/F
485 showed a good linear relationship with C
Hg
2+ in the range of 0.02–2.0 μM (Fig. 3b). The linear equation F
678
/F
485 = −1.17788C
Hg
2+ + 2.70789 was obtained with a correlation coefficient (R
2 ) of 0.9979. In addition, the detection limit of 5.7 nM for Hg2+ is comparable with or even higher than that of most reported approaches for Hg2+ detection (Table S2). This is because the ratiometric probe cloud eliminates numerous ambiguous effects through self-calibration compared with the single-emission fluorescent probe (Wu et al., 2023). Furthermore, abundant electronegative atoms, particularly the sulfur-containing groups, show stronger coordination with Hg2+ than HgC or HgN formation between Hg2+ and the probe ( Mermer, Yavuz, Atasen, Alcay, & Yilmaz, 2021; Oh et al., 2020).

Selective recognition of fluorescent probes is an essential basis of their practical applications. Therefore, the selectivity and anti-interference behaviour of de-CDs for Hg2+ detection were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the value of F
678
/F
485 obviously decreased upon introducing Hg2+ into the de-CDs system, whereas there was no significant change after introducing other anions and cations. Nevertheless, when Hg2+ coexists with other ions, the value of F
678
/F
485 obtained by de-CDs is almost identical to that obtained when Hg2+ is introduced alone. These results suggest that the as-synthesised de-CDs possess excellent selectivity and anti-interference for Hg2+ determination. This satisfactory specificity can be attributed to the strong and specific coordination of Hg2+ with de-CDs. These findings demonstrate that the ratiometric detection of Hg2+ based on de-CDs is feasible and satisfactory.

Note that the de-CDs show a significant fluorescence response to Cd2+, and the response signal is comparable to that of Hg2+ (Fig. S10a, b). As shown in Fig. S11a, the UV–vis absorption intensity of de-CDs decreased significantly after adding Cd2+, which is similar to Hg2+. Moreover, when the concentration of Cd2+ reached 2.0 μM, a significant shift in the UV–vis absorption spectra was observed, further confirming the strong interaction of ground-state de-CDs with Cd2+. However, the remarkable difference between the de-CD–Hg2+ and de-CDs–Cd2+ systems is that the fluorescence of de-CDs quenched by Cd2+ cannot be recovered after the introduction of malathion. This is possibly due to the stronger coordination of de-CDs with Cd2+ than that of malathion with Cd2+ (Fig. S11b). In addition, the Cd2+ concentration in food substrates and the environment is generally extremely low, which will not significantly interfere with OPs determination. Therefore, the de-CD–Hg2+ complex can function as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for OPs determination even in the presence of Cd2+.

The aforementioned results prompted us to investigate the target responsiveness of the de-CD–Hg2+ complex to omethoate, diazinon and malathion. To investigate the detection sensitivity of OPs, the fluorescence response of the de-CD–Hg2+ complex to OPs at different concentrations was examined. As displayed in Fig. 4a–c, the fluorescence signal at 678 nm gradually enhanced with increasing OPs concentration, whereas the emission intensity at 485 nm showed a negligible change, thereby resulting in an obvious change in F
678
/F
485 . A calibration curve was plotted using F
678
/F
485 and OPs concentration as the ordinate and abscissa, respectively (Fig. 4d–f). Accordingly, omethoate, diazinon and malathion have quantitative ranges of 0.01–8, 0.02–10 and 0.02–10 μg⋅mL−1, respectively. Their detection limits are 2.8, 5.4 and 6.1 ng⋅mL−1, which are well below their MRLs in food samples (GB-2763–2016) (Li et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021). Furthermore, the sensitivity of the probe is comparable with or even higher than that of previously reported approaches for OPs detection (Table S3). This reliable performance is predominantly attributed to the dual-emission property of de-CDs and their excellent response to Hg2+ or OPs at trace levels. More importantly, the proposed sensing platform avoids the incorporation of additional enzymes or aptamers, thereby simplifying the fabrication process and improving the stability of the probe, which will contribute to accurate detection of targets.

Subsequently, the fluorescence response of the de-CD–Hg2+ complex to other OPs or possible interfering substances was investigated. To this end, other OPs, including acephate, dipterex, parathion-methyl, dichlorvos, phoxim and chlorpyrifos, as well as the carbamate insecticide methomyl, were added to the de-CD–Hg2+ system instead of omethoate, diazinon and malathion. No obvious change was observed in F
678
/F
485 when the other OPs or non-OPs were introduced (Fig. 4g). In addition, the fluorescence response to tetracycline, glucose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, oxalic acid, l-alanine, l-leucine, l-glutathione and l-lysine, which may be present with OPs in agricultural crops, was examined to check the selectivity. Furthermore, the anti-interference behaviour of the probe was investigated by mixing malathion with other interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 4h, the fluorescence response of the de-CD–Hg2+ system to the coexistence of malathion with other pesticides or possible interfering substances is comparable with that when malathion is added alone. The reason the probe responds to omethoate, diazinon and malathion but not to other OPs or non-OPs could be the synergistic effect of electronegative S, N and O atoms in the three OPs that coordinate with Hg2+ (Oh et al., 2020). It is known that the electronegative S atom shows the strongest coordination with Hg2+. In addition, electronegative N and O atoms can easily coordinate with Hg2+. In particular, the four electronegative atoms coordinated with P can form a stable network through coordination with Hg2+, resulting in significant fluorescence recovery of the probe at 678 nm. The chemical structures of the OPs are shown in Figs. 4a-c and S12. For the other OPs and methomyl, their weak coordination with Hg2+ results in a negligible probe response due to the free rotation of benzene and the electronic push–pull ability of halogen atoms (Wang et al., 2016).

Currently, several fluorescent probes have been developed for OPs detection with high selectivity and strong anti-interference ability, and good accuracy has been achieved for OPs detection in extraction from various vegetables without thorough purification (Cai et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020; Li et al., 2021; Vadia et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021). Compared with other probes, the proposed de-CD–Hg2+ complex probe avoids the incorporation of additional enzymes or aptamers and simplifies the preparation process, thereby reducing the cost and improving the stability of fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent assay of Hg2+ and OPs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: From a practical perspective, the detection of Hg2+ and OPs in environmental water and agricultural food samples was performed using the proposed fluorescent probe. Real samples, including tap water and lake water, were chosen as the sample matrix to investigate the applicability of de-CDs for Hg2+ determination. Different concentrations of Hg2+ (20, 200 and 1000 nM) were added into the real samples. The spiked samples were analysed using the probe, and the determination results are described in Table S4. Satisfactory recoveries of 90.5%–98.9% were achieved, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were below 5.4%. Furthermore, the detection results of the proposed method agree well with those of ICP–MS, confirming the potential applicability of de-CDs for Hg2+ assay in real samples.

To explore the practicability of the de-CD–Hg2+ probe for OPs detection in the food matrix, extract solutions from two groups of Brassica chinensis (one group regularly sprayed with omethoate during their growth and another group not sprayed with omethoate served as a contrast) were determined. As shown in Fig. 5a, b, no significant difference in appearance was observed between the two groups of Brassica chinensis. The extraction solution from the leaves and roots of the unsprayed Brassica chinensis was diluted 100-fold before determination. After adding the two diluted extraction solutions to the probe solution, no obvious fluorescence change was observed (Fig. S13a). Moreover, no signal of omethoate was observed in the detection result of HPLC (Fig. S13b). It is worth noting that ratiometric detection can eliminate numerous ambiguous effects through self-calibration, effectively shielding background interference or eliminating the ‘matrix effect’ in the sample matrix. Therefore, the standard addition method was performed by adding omethoate (0.05, 1.0 and 5.0 μg⋅mL−1) to the diluted extract solutions, and the recovery of omethoate was determined based on the established solvent-based standard calibration in Fig. 4d. As shown in Table S5, a recovery of 90.0–102.4% with RSDs less than 5.8% was obtained for omethoate determination in the spiked extraction solution. Good recoveries with low RSDs demonstrate the potential applicability of the proposed probe for OPs detection in realistic samples.

In addition, as the dilution ratio of the extraction solution from the sprayed Brassica chinensis increased, the peak area of omethoate in HPLC decreased, proving its presence in the extraction solution (Fig. S14). To determine the omethoate residue, the extraction solution of leaves from the unsprayed Brassica chinensis was used as a blank matrix to prepare the matrix-matched calibration solution to eliminate the ‘matrix effect’ in HPLC determination (Poole, 2007; Rahman et al., 2018; Yarita, Aoyagi, & Otake, 2015). Calibration curves were created for the following concentrations (three replicates per level): 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5 and 1 mgꞏL−1. Peak area versus concentration was plotted. As shown in Fig. S15, a matrix-matched calibration curve was obtained with a determination coefficient (R2) = 0.99989. Finally, omethoate in the extraction solution of the sprayed Brassica chinensis was detected based on the calibration curve (Table 1 ). Furthermore, the omethoate residue in the sprayed Brassica chinensis was determined using the de-CD–Hg2+ probe. As shown in Fig. 5c–h, the fluorescence at 678 nm noticeably increased with the introduction of the extraction solution. Moreover, weaker fluorescence was observed with increasing dilution ratio (Table S6). The residue levels of omethoate in leaves and roots were determined to be 5.26 and 13.50 μg.g−1, respectively, by detecting the extraction solution diluted 100-fold based on the established matrix-free calibration curve in Fig. 4d (Table 1). More significantly, the proposed probe was used to determine the variation of omethoate residues in the leaves and roots of Brassica chinensis on different days from the last spray of omethoate. The height of Brassica chinensis during growth was visually assessed. At 15 days after sowing, Brassica chinensis reached a height of approximately 2 cm. Afterwards, OPs were sprayed every 3 days. After 10 sprays, Brassica chinensis grow to approximately 7–10 cm. The residue level of omethoate in the leaves and roots decreased with prolonged time, and after the last spray for 10 days, no omethoate residue was detected in the leaves. All results agree well with those detected by HPLC, demonstrating the potential application of the proposed probe for accurate and simple monitoring of OPs residues. Nevertheless, it may be necessary to evaluate its performance in determining OPs in multiple vegetables for future applications.

In summary, intrinsic de-CDs were developed using a simple hydrothermal process for the ratiometric determination of OPs and Hg2+. The de-CDs not only showed high FLQY values of 15.1% and 12.0% at 678 and 485 nm, respectively, when excited with visible light but also exhibited a particular pH-responsive property. More importantly, based on the de-CDs, a ratiometric determination of three OPs (omethoate, diazinon and malathion) and Hg2+ with high selectivity and anti-interference was achieved. The quantitative concentration of the proposed probe was 0.01–8 μg⋅mL−1 for omethoate, 0.02–10 μg⋅mL−1 for diazinon, 0.02–10 μg⋅mL−1 for malathion and 0.02–2.0 μM for Hg2+. Finally, the de-CDs were successfully applied to detect OPs and Hg2+ in vegetable and water samples. The detection results of OPs residues in Brassica chinensis agree well with those obtained by HPLC. Compared with previously reported probes for the OPs assay, the proposed probe avoids the incorporation of additional enzymes or aptamers and simplifies the probe preparation process, thereby reducing the cost and improving the stability of fluorescent probes. Nevertheless, the detection of OPs has only been verified in Brassica chinensis. It may be necessary to evaluate its performance in detecting OPs in multiple vegetables in the future. In summary, this work not only provides a simple and cost-effective strategy for monitoring OPs residues but also offers new insights into the fabrication of multi-functional carbon dots with promising applications.

Huifang Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Qiqi Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Miaochen Dong: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Xinyue Liu: Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Yanfeng Tang: Supervision, Resources, Project administration.
